en.extract:
The white-bellied treepie (Dendrocitta leucogastra) is a bird of the crow family endemic to the forests of southern India. They overlap in distribution in some areas with the rufous treepie but are easy to tell apart both from appearance and call.


Description
The white of the head and body makes it easy to distinguish from the sympatric rufous treepie. This tends to be found in more dense forest and is less associated with human habitation than the rufous treepie. The white-bellied treepie is 48 cm (19 in) long. The back of the neck is white, and the throat and breast are black. The thighs are black, and the undertail coverts are chestnut. The rest of the underparts is white. The back is chestnut-brown. The wings are black and have a white patch. The rump is white. Two-thirds of the two central tail feathers are silver-grey, and the terminal third is black. The other tail feathers are black. The beak is black, and the legs are greyish-black.


Distribution
It is found in the forests of the Western Ghats mainly south of Goa. A record from Erimalai near Dharmapuri and reports from the Surat Dangs and the southeastern Ghats of Andhra Pradesh stand outside its main distribution range. A record from central India (Chikalda, Gawilgarh) has been questioned.


Behaviour and ecology
The white-bellied treepie eats fruits, seeds, nectar, invertebrates, reptiles, rodents, nestlings and eggs. When calling, the bird bows and droops its wings. Several birds may arrive at one tree and call repeatedly during the pre-monsoon breeding season (mainly April–May but some nests from February). The nest is a platform of twigs on a medium-sized tree. Three eggs are laid, ashy grey with green and grey blotches.
It is associated with mixed-species foraging flocks and is often found along with greater racket-tailed drongos.


Gallery

The '''white-bellied treepie''' ('''''Dendrocitta leucogastra''''') is a bird of the [[crow]] family endemic to the forests of southern India. They overlap in distribution in some areas with the [[rufous treepie]] but are easy to tell apart both from appearance and call.

== Description ==
The white of the head and body makes it easy to distinguish from the sympatric [[rufous treepie]]. This tends to be found in more dense forest and is less associated with human habitation than the rufous treepie.<ref name=pcr/> The white-bellied treepie is {{convert|48|cm|abbr=on}} long.<ref name=Madge /> The back of the neck is white, and the throat and breast are black. The thighs are black, and the undertail coverts are chestnut. The rest of the underparts is white. The back is chestnut-brown. The wings are black and have a white patch. The rump is white. Two-thirds of the two central tail feathers are silver-grey, and the terminal third is black. The other tail feathers are black. The beak is black, and the legs are greyish-black.<ref name=Madge>{{cite book|last1=Madge|first1=Steve|title=Crows and Jays|date=2010|publisher=A&C Black|isbn=978-1-4081-3169-5|page=116}}</ref>

== Distribution ==
It is found in the forests of the [[Western Ghats]] mainly south of Goa.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1073/pnas.89.12.5311|author=Daniels, R J Ranjit|author2=NV Joshi|author3=Madhav Gadgil|name-list-style=amp |year=1992 |title= On the relationship between bird and woody plant species diversity in the Uttara Kannada district of south India|journal= Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. |volume=89|issue=12|pages=5311–5315 |url=http://www.pnas.org/content/89/12/5311.full.pdf?ck=nck|pmid=11607298|pmc=49281 |bibcode=1992PNAS...89.5311D|doi-access=free}}</ref> A record from Erimalai near Dharmapuri<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Daniels, R.J.R.  |author2=MV Ravikumar |name-list-style=amp |year=1997|title=Birds of Erimalai|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=37|issue=5|pages=80–82|url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW37_5#page/n11/mode/2up/}}</ref> and reports from the Surat Dangs and the southeastern Ghats of Andhra Pradesh stand outside its main distribution range. A record from central India (Chikalda, Gawilgarh<ref name=fbi/>) has been questioned.<ref name=pcr>{{cite book|author1=Rasmussen, PC  |author2=JC Anderton  |name-list-style=amp |year=2005|title= Birds of South Asia: The Ripley Guide. Volume 2|publisher=Smithsonian Institution & Lynx Edicions|page=596}}</ref>

==Behaviour and ecology==
The white-bellied treepie eats fruits, seeds, nectar, invertebrates, reptiles, rodents, nestlings and eggs.<ref name=Madge /> When calling, the bird bows and droops its wings. Several birds may arrive at one tree and call repeatedly during the pre-[[monsoon]] breeding season (mainly April–May but some nests from February). The nest is a platform of twigs on a medium-sized tree. Three eggs are laid, ashy grey with green and grey blotches.<ref>{{cite book|author=Hume, A O|author-link=Allan Octavian Hume| year=1889|title=The Nests and Eggs of Indian Birds. Volume 1|page= 22|url=https://archive.org/stream/nestseggsofindia01humerich#page/22/mode/2up/search/leucogastra|publisher=R H Porter, London}}</ref><ref name=fbi>{{cite book|author=Baker, ECS|year=1922|title= The Fauna of British India, Including Ceylon and Burma. Birds. Volume 1| publisher=Taylor and Francis, London |pages=51–52|edition=2nd|url=https://archive.org/stream/faunaofbritishin01bake#page/52/mode/2up}}</ref>

It is associated with [[mixed-species foraging flock]]s and is often found along with [[greater racket-tailed drongo]]s.<ref name=pcr/>

zh.extract:
白腹树鹊（学名：Dendrocitta leucogastra），是鸦科树鹊属的一种，为印度的特有种。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
白腹树鹊的平均体重约为99.1克。栖息地包括种植园、亚热带或热带的湿润低地林、亚热带或热带严重退化的前森林和亚热带或热带的湿润山地林。


参考文献
